result.coverage_notes:
Deep second-pass audit of coinbase::mpc::ecdsampc::sign (src/cbmpc/protocol/ecdsa_mp.cpp, the only signing path under public APIs sign_additive/sign_ac in include/cbmpc/api/ecdsa_mp.h). Read the signing function in full (lines 37-486) plus the ZK proof verifiers in src/cbmpc/zk/zk_elgamal_com.cpp. ot_role_map is validated up front (lines 46-64) so the c_api/demo entry points cannot inject malformed maps reaching OOB.

Received-value -> validation confirmed:
- h_consistency._j (round 1->2): checked equal across parties (line 94), binding all signers to the same (msg, Q, Qis). Local key consistency also enforced: x_share*G==Qi (73) and SUM(Qis)==Q (74).
- sid_i._js / c._js: sid derived by hashing sorted pids + all sid_i (99-101); commitment c opened and verified against Ei_gen in round 2 (line 145, commitment_t.open).
- Ei_gen._j (ElGamal key DKG): commitment-opened (145) AND zk uc_dl proof pi_s verified (147) before E = SUM(E_i) is formed (150). Curve validity checked inside verify.
- ot_msg1/msg2/msg3 (PVW base-OT + extension): base.output_R does curve.check on U0/U1 (ot.cpp 89-90); OT correlation correctness is later cross-checked by the in-exponent MtA proofs (below), not trusted directly.
- v_theta._j (OT-part round 4->5): explicit range check q.is_in_range on every component (lines 289-292) before use; out-of-range rejected with E_CRYPTO.
- eK_i._j, eRHO_i._j (round 3->4): each accompanied by uc_elgamal_com proof pi_eK/pi_eRHO, verified (236-237) with full curve.check + Fischlin range checks (zk_elgamal_com.cpp 64-115) BEFORE eK/eRHO are reused (e.g. eB built at 449 from already-verified eRHO_i._j).
- eRHO_K._j, eRHO_X._j, F_eRHO_K._j, F_eRHO_X._j (round 5->6): bound by uc_elgamal_com proofs (360-361) and elgamal_com_mult proofs pi_F_* (358-359) tying F = rho * commitment; verifiers check curves and Schnorr-style equations (zk_elgamal_com.cpp 154-176). h (transcript hash) checked equal (386).
- Z_eRHO_K_i._j, Z_eRHO_X_i._j (round 6->7): uc_elgamal_com_mult_private_scalar proofs verified (388-391); h2 transcript equality checked (418).
- W_eRHO_K_i._j, W_eRHO_X_i._j, K_i._j (round 7->8): DH proofs pi_W_* and elgamal_com_pub_share_equ proof pi_K verified (419-421). Critical MtA-integrity gate: W_eRHO_K==Z_eRHO_K.R and W_eRHO_X==Z_eRHO_X.R enforced (431-432) — this is the consistency check tying the recombined rho*k / rho*x products to the committed nonce/share, blocking a malicious signer from injecting an inconsistent product.
- rho_k._j, beta._j (final all-to-one, round 8->receiver): each bound to a verified commitment via elgamal_com_pub_share_equ proofs pi_R_eRHO_K (RHO_K=rho_k_i*G vs eRHO_K) and pi_R_eB (B=beta*G vs eB) verified on the receiver (469-470). eB[j] itself is a deterministic affine combination of already-proven eRHO_X._j and eRHO_i._j.
- Final signature: receiver recomputes (r,s), low-s normalizes (478-479), and re-verifies against key.Q with pub.verify(msg,sig) (482) — any forged/biased aggregate is rejected here, and non-receiver parties send no secret-bearing value in the clear that isn't commitment-bound.

Nonce handling: k_i, rho_i are fresh bn_t::rand(q) per signer per invocation; no reuse across the protocol and no caller-influenced seed. K is committed (eK_i) and proven before r is derived, so a malicious party cannot adaptively bias the nonce after seeing others' K_i (commit-then-open ordering via the round structure and pi_K). No exploitable missing check found; result is zero findings.</coverage_notes>
</invoke>

Surface audited: the ZK proofs consumed by mp-ECDSA DKG/signing reachable from public APIs coinbase::api::ecdsa_mp (dkg_additive/dkg_ac/refresh_*/sign_additive/sign_ac), which dispatch to coinbase::mpc::ecdsampc::sign and coinbase::mpc::eckey::key_share_mp_t::{dkg,refresh,dkg_or_refresh_ac}. Read in full: src/cbmpc/protocol/ecdsa_mp.cpp, src/cbmpc/protocol/ec_dkg.cpp, src/cbmpc/zk/zk_ec.cpp, src/cbmpc/zk/zk_elgamal_com.cpp, src/cbmpc/zk/fischlin.cpp, plus include-internal zk_ec.h and ro.h.

Proofs consumed and confirmed properly verified (each verify() is invoked at the consumption site with a LOCALLY-reconstructed public statement, never a prover-supplied one):
- uc_dl_t (zk_ec.cpp): DKG ec_dkg.cpp:157 verify(Qi._j, sid, j) where Qi._j is opened from the sid_i/pid-bound commitment (line 154); refresh ec_dkg.cpp:230 verify(R._j[l], sid, j*n+l); signing pi_s ecdsa_mp.cpp:147 verify(Ei_gen._j, sid, peers_count+j) after commitment open at :145. Fiat-Shamir hash (zk_ec.cpp:33/73) binds G, Q, A, session_id, aux. Curve membership of Q and A[i] checked at verify (:66-68). z range-checked check_right_open_range(0,z,q) (:80). Fischlin e implicitly bound by the b_mask hash gate (:89). Sound.
- uc_batch_dl_t (zk_ec.cpp:214): dkg_ac/refresh_ac ec_dkg.cpp:390 verify(Rs, sid, j) after commitment open :386; per-Q curve check :234, R[i] curve check :241, z range check :251, R[i]==z*G-horner(PQ,e) algebraic check :256.
- dh_t (zk_ec.cpp:285): used via elgamal_com_pub_share_equ_t and directly (pi_W_eRHO_K/X). Verify checks Q,A,B on curve (:291-293), e and z in [0,q) (:298-299), recomputes e' and compares (:305). Hash binds G,Q,A,B,X,Y,session_id,aux.
- uc_elgamal_com_t (zk_elgamal_com.cpp:64): signing pi_eK/pi_eRHO/pi_eRHO_K/pi_eRHO_X verified at ecdsa_mp.cpp:236-237,360-361 against received eK_i._j/eRHO_K._j/eRHO_X._j and locally-fixed E. Curve checks on Q, UV, AB (:75-78); z1,z2 range checks (:93-94); two algebraic batch identities (:111-112).
- elgamal_com_mult_t (zk_elgamal_com.cpp:154): pi_F_eRHO_K/X verified ecdsa_mp.cpp:358-359 with A=eK/eX recomputed locally and B=eRHO_i._j received, C=F_eRHO_*._j received. Curve checks A,B,C,Q; e,z1,z2,z3 range checks; e==e' check.
- uc_elgamal_com_mult_private_scalar_t (zk_elgamal_com.cpp:239): pi_Z_eRHO_K/X verified ecdsa_mp.cpp:388-390 against locally-computed Y_eRHO_* and received Z_*._j. Curve checks on Q,A,B and per-i A1_tag/A2_tag; z range checks; two algebraic identities.
- elgamal_com_pub_share_equ_t (zk_elgamal_com.cpp:124): pi_K, pi_R_eRHO_K, pi_R_eB verified at ecdsa_mp.cpp:421,469-470; A reconstructed locally (K_i._j, RHO_K=rho_k._j*G, B=beta._j*G), reduces to dh_t.verify which performs full curve/range/hash checks.

Session/transcript binding confirmed: signing sid = sha256(all parties' broadcast sid_i, sorted pids) (ecdsa_mp.cpp:99-101), binding session id, all party contributions, and party identities; aux slots are per-party-disjoint (n_*_proofs * j + offset), preventing intra-session proof reuse across slots. DKG sid = sha256(all sid_i) with aux=party index and commitment bound to (sid_i, pid); a replayed/copied proof fails because the statement Q in the FS hash differs and the witness is unknown. Cross-session replay is prevented because sid is fresh per run (sid_i are freshly random bitlen SEC_P_COM). Commitments (commitment_t) bind sid_i and pid before opening (ecdsa_mp.cpp:145, ec_dkg.cpp:154/227).

Non-proof received values also confirmed validated: h_consistency equality across parties (msg,Q,Qis) ecdsa_mp.cpp:94; v_theta range check q.is_in_range (:291); x_share*G==Qi and SUM(Qis)==Q sanity (:73-74); W_eRHO_*==Z.R checks (:431-432); final pub.verify(msg,sig) on sig_receiver (:482); refresh x_share/Qi/Q consistency (ec_dkg.cpp:184-186,257-259); ac dkg reconstruct_exponent==Q and x_i*G==Qis[i] (:432-434).

No instance found where a value received from a counterparty feeds a key/signature computation without the required proof-verify or algebraic consistency check, and no verify() call supplied a prover-controlled statement. Result: zero exploitable findings.</coverage_notes>
</invoke>

Deep second-pass audit of the PVE-AC + ElGamal surface anchored to include/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_ac.h. ZERO exploitable findings; the surface is soundly defended. Received values confirmed properly validated:

1. Ciphertext blob (verify_ac/combine_ac/partial_decrypt_ac_attempt): parse_ac_ciphertext (pve_batch_ac.cpp:29-37) enforces version==1, batch_count in [1, MAX_BATCH_COUNT=100000]. MAX_CIPHERTEXT_BLOB_SIZE bound applied before parse.

2. Q points embedded in ciphertext (pve_ct.get_Q()): deserialized via ecc_point_t::convert_fixed_curve (base_ecc.cpp:737) which calls from_bin THEN curve.check (base_ecc.cpp:755). ecurve_t::check (base_ecc.cpp:586) enforces valid/correct-curve/IS-IN-SUBGROUP/non-infinity. So on-curve + subgroup validation IS enforced at deserialization even though combine_ac (line 336) only re-checks curve id. verify_ac additionally re-checks via icurve.check (line 193). No invalid-curve / small-subgroup vector.

3. Expected Qs_compressed (verify_ac): each from_bin + icurve.check (pve_batch_ac.cpp:192-193); count matched to batch_count (line 178); and verify() (pve_ac.cpp:144-148) requires Q==this->Q exactly and re-checks curve.check per element.

4. label binding: L = sha256(label, Q) recomputed and compared (pve_ac.cpp:150-151), bound into AES-GCM AAD and into the Fiat-Shamir hash b.

5. Access structure (ac): shape-validated (access_structure_util.h validate_access_structure_node, depth<=128, nodes<=4096, threshold 1<=k<=n), leaf set must EXACTLY equal party set (to_internal_access_structure line 180), internal tree re-validated (validate_tree, secret_sharing.cpp:57). Deserialized ac_owned_t::convert also runs validate_tree.

6. Leaf key set (ac_pks): validate_leaf_keys_exact requires exact bijection with leaf names, non-empty names, size bounds (pve_batch_ac.cpp:54-76).

7. quorum_shares (combine_ac): validate_quorum_shares_subset (names known, size bounds) PLUS exact share size == curve order byte size (line 340-343) before from_bin.

8. Cut-and-choose soundness (verify): for each of kappa=128 rows, the opened side's leaf/quorum ciphertexts are re-encrypted deterministically (seed-derived randomness) and the full transcript (Q,label,c0,c1,quorum_c0,quorum_c1,X0,X1) re-hashed and compared to committed b (pve_ac.cpp:189-191). Soundness error 2^-128.

9. Combine wrong-secret protection: reconstruct (secret_sharing.cpp:478) -> K; AES-GCM tag verifies; final per-batch check x[j]*G == Q[j] (pve_ac.cpp:285) returns E_CRYPTO on any mismatch. A malicious quorum party supplying a corrupted share yields wrong K and is caught here — verification cannot pass while decryption yields a wrong secret. The documented "combine does not verify ciphertext" caveat is safe: it only guarantees output is the dlog of the ciphertext's own Q; callers are instructed to call verify_ac first.

10. ElGamal (elgamal.cpp): this file is the ec_elgamal_commitment_t class (commitment arithmetic), not directly reachable as an entry through pve_batch_ac.h; all points it operates on are ecc_point_t already subgroup-validated at construction/deserialization. ct_add constant-time guard (cb_assert_elgamal_ct_curve) present. No unvalidated point ingress tied to the PVE-AC public path.

Conclusion: every received value on the PVE-AC public-API boundary that feeds decryption/reconstruction is validated (subgroup check at deserialization is the key defense), and the final x*G==Q gate makes malformed-quorum-share / wrong-secret attacks fail closed for an honest combiner. No Medium+ independent-parties PoC exists.

DEEP second-pass audit of multi-party ECDSA DKG reachable from public APIs in include/cbmpc/api/ecdsa_mp.h: dkg_additive -> ecdsampc::dkg -> eckey::key_share_mp_t::dkg (src/cbmpc/protocol/ec_dkg.cpp:112-165); dkg_ac/refresh_ac -> key_share_mp_t::dkg_or_refresh_ac (ec_dkg.cpp:264-460); refresh_additive -> key_share_mp_t::refresh (ec_dkg.cpp:167-262). Note: ecdsa_mp.cpp sign() is the signing protocol, not DKG; DKG itself contains NO Paillier material (none received), so no Paillier-modulus/ciphertext attack surface exists on this path.

Received-value -> validation table (all confirmed PROPERLY validated):

ADDITIVE DKG (key_share_mp_t::dkg):
- h_consistency (curve binding): cross-checked equal across parties, ec_dkg.cpp:136. OK.
- sid_i (peer session id): broadcast plaintext round1 together with commitment c; sid=H(all sid_i); honest commitment c is fixed in round1 over honest party's own sid_i, so a rushing/adaptive choice of malicious sid_i gives no binding advantage. OK.
- c (commitment hash): opened in round2 via commitment_t(sid_i._j, pid_j).set(rho._j,c._j).open(Qi._j), ec_dkg.cpp:154 — commitment bound to peer pid (job.get_pid(j)) and peer sid_i. OK.
- sid_msg._j: checked == locally derived sid, ec_dkg.cpp:151. OK.
- h._j: checked == H(c._js), ec_dkg.cpp:152. OK.
- Qi._j (peer public share): commitment-opened AND passed to uc_dl_t::verify (zk_ec.cpp:55) which runs curve.check(Q) = valid + correct-curve + in-subgroup + non-infinity (base_ecc.cpp:586-595), and the DL proof binds Qi to aux=j (peer index) and sid. So on-curve/subgroup/non-identity + proof-of-knowledge + per-party binding all enforced before SUM into key.Q. OK.
- pi._j (uc_dl Fischlin proof): structural size checks (rho), curve.check on all A[i], z[i] range check via check_right_open_range(0,z,q), Fischlin hash==0 and aggregate A_sum==z_sum*G-e_sum*Q (zk_ec.cpp:79-93). Statement bound to (G,Q,A,sid,aux=j). OK.

AC DKG/refresh (dkg_or_refresh_ac):
- h_consistency (curve_name,all_pids,quorum_pids,sid): cross-checked, ec_dkg.cpp:308. OK.
- c_all._j (per-party commitment): opened via com_R_tag bound to quorum_pids[j] over (Rs, pi_r_all._j), ec_dkg.cpp:385-386; Rs rebuilt deterministically from the (std::map-ordered) ac_pub_all._j so the whole public-share map is commitment-bound. OK.
- ac_pub_all._j (internal+root public points): every point covered by the batch-DL proof (Rs spans the full map) and by reconstruct_exponent's curve.check (secret_sharing.cpp:558-560). OK.
- pi_r_all._j (uc_batch_dl): verify does curve.check on all Q[j] and R[i], z range checks, R[i]==z*G-horner_poly(PQ,e) per index, Fischlin hash==0 (zk_ec.cpp:214-265); bound to aux=j and sid. OK.
- xij._j (secret additive share received by this party): validated by ac.verify_share_against_ancestors_pub_data(Qj, xij._j, ac_pub_all._j, name(i)) (secret_sharing.cpp:344-408) — full Feldman/VSS ancestor chain check si*G up the tree to root Qj, covering LEAF/OR/AND/THRESHOLD (Lagrange-in-exponent) consistency. OK.
- h_all._j: cross-checked vs representative and recomputed by non-quorum parties (ec_dkg.cpp:376,400). OK.
- Global consistency: reconstruct_exponent(Qis)==Q (ec_dkg.cpp:430-432) and x_i*G==Qis[self] (434), plus refresh's reconstruct==key.Q (447) bind the assembled key to validated material. OK.

REFRESH (additive, key_share_mp_t::refresh):
- input key self-consistency checked (x_share*G==Qi, SUM(Qis)==Q) ec_dkg.cpp:184-186.
- c._j commitment opened over (R._j, pi_r._j) bound to peer pid (227); batch sizes checked (224-225); each R._j[l] proven via uc_dl verify incl. curve.check (230); pairwise r._ji*G==R._j[i] (232). Final new_key self-checks: Qis[self]==x_share*G (257) and SUM(Qis)==current Q (259). OK.

Minor non-finding noted: in dkg_or_refresh_ac, ac_pub_all._j.at(name) (ec_dkg.cpp:394,410,423) would throw std::out_of_range if a malicious quorum member omits a map key — a possible abort/crash, but a malicious party can already abort the protocol by non-participation; no key compromise, no honest-party security impact. Not eligible.

Result: ZERO exploitable findings. Every value a DKG party accepts from a counterparty is gated by an opened+pid-bound commitment, an on-curve/in-subgroup/non-identity check, a per-party-bound ZK proof of knowledge, and (for AC) a Feldman-VSS ancestor-consistency check plus a global reconstruct_exponent==Q equality. No received value is used without its required check.

Deep second-pass audit of PVE verification soundness. Read in full: src/cbmpc/protocol/pve.cpp, pve_base.cpp, pve_batch.cpp; include/cbmpc/api/pve_base_pke.h, pve_batch_single_recipient.h; the API implementations src/cbmpc/api/pve_base_pke.cpp and pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp; and internal headers pve.h, pve_batch.h, pve_base.h. Result: ZERO findings. The construction's soundness binding is solid.

Core soundness conclusion: For both base (ec_pve_t) and batch (ec_pve_batch_t), the public decrypt/decrypt_batch APIs are always invoked with skip_verify=true and rely on the final discrete-log check in restore_from_decrypted: base pve.cpp:140 `if (x_value * G != Q) {x_value=0; return E_CRYPTO;}` and batch pve_batch.cpp:183 `if (Q[i] != x[i]*G) return E_CRYPTO;`. This check binds whatever bytes the base PKE decryption returns to the EXACT discrete log of the verified Q. A malicious encryptor therefore cannot make decryption silently return a different/zero/garbage scalar than committed: any deviation fails x*G==Q and is rejected (out_x set to 0 / out_xs cleared, error returned). No soundness break (wrong-plaintext acceptance) is reachable.

Received values I confirmed are properly validated:
- Q / Qs (caller-supplied expected point): on-curve via curve.check (pve.cpp:73, pve_batch.cpp:84); equals stored Q via `Q != this->Q` (pve.cpp:74, pve_batch.cpp:86); curve match enforced in API (pve_base_pke.cpp:233, pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:119-120); decoded from compressed bin with from_bin + check (pve_base_pke.cpp:236-237).
- label: matched against stored L (pve.cpp:75, pve_batch.cpp:87, and again in batch decrypt pve_batch.cpp:195); bound into inner_label via genPVELabelWithPoint (SHA-256 of point) and into the Fiat-Shamir hash.
- b (challenge / commitment): is a fixed-width buf128_t (kappa=SEC_P_COM=128), so get_bit(i) is always in-range; constrained to equal H(Q,label,c0,c1,X0,X1) via b_tag==b (pve.cpp:114, pve_batch.cpp:140). Encrypt uses .bitlen(kappa); batch verify uses .bitlen(SEC_P_COM) — confirmed equal (both 128), so no truncation mismatch.
- x_rows / x_bin (opened scalars): base bounds x_rows[i].get_bin_size()<=max_scalar_size and q.is_in_range (pve.cpp:88-89, 130-131); batch enforces rows[i].x_bin.size()==n*curve_size (pve_batch.cpp:108,159) and vector_from_bin range-checks against q.
- r (opened randomness): batch enforces exact sizes (16 for bi=1 at pve_batch.cpp:107; 32 for bi=0 at 117,165); base r is fixed buf128_t.
- decrypted_x_buf: size-checked before use (base pve.cpp:124 <=max_scalar_size, q.is_in_range:126; batch r01.size==16 at pve_batch.cpp:162, x1_bin.size==n*curve_size at 168).
- batch_count / n: bounded (0,MAX_BATCH_COUNT=100000] at parse (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:39-41) and re-checked in verify (pve_batch.cpp:77); Q.size()==n and Qs_compressed.size()==n enforced.
- stored ciphertext c[i]/rows[i].c: bound into the Fiat-Shamir hash (the unopened half is the cut-and-choose witness, deliberately not re-encrypted in verify); correctness of the unopened half is enforced at decrypt time by x*G==Q. Probability of verify-passing-but-all-rows-undecryptable is 2^-128 (standard cut-and-choose).
- API memory args: ek/dk/ciphertext size-bounded (MAX_OPAQUE_BLOB_SIZE / MAX_CIPHERTEXT_BLOB_SIZE); x/xs element size <= curve order size; version field checked ==1.
- key type / curve in base PKE bridge: validated (ECIES must be P-256), invalid key -> E_BADARG.

Deterministic-rho property (header contract pve_base_pke.h:11-15) is honored by the impl (pve_base.cpp:13 seeds drbg from rho) so verify's re-encryption reproduces the committed ciphertext bytes for the opened half; mismatch flips the hash and fails b_tag==b. No gap found.

findings.title: Batch PVE decrypt: attacker-controlled inner Q vector length is not re-checked against batch_count, causing out-of-bounds std::vector read of ecc_point_t (wild EC_POINT* deref) on the honest decryptor
findings.public_api_entry: coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch(curve_id, dk, ek, ciphertext, label, out_xs) in include/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_single_recipient.h
findings.vuln_class: Out-of-bounds read / missing length validation on deserialized container
findings.missing_check: After deserializing ec_pve_batch_t from the untrusted blob, nothing verifies that the inner length-prefixed Q vector (read by ec_pve_batch_t::convert -> converter.convert(Q,...)) has size == n. ec_pve_batch_t::convert (include-internal/cbmpc/internal/protocol/pve_batch.h:48-61) checks `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` BEFORE reading, when Q is still the constructor-sized vector, so the check is a no-op; the subsequent std::vector overload does Q.clear()/resize(attacker_count), making Q.size() fully attacker-controlled. verify() (pve_batch.cpp:78) does enforce Q.size()==n, but the decrypt path runs with skip_verify=true and never re-validates. restore_from_decrypted() validates row_index but not Q.size().
findings.data_flow: decrypt_batch -> parse_batch_ciphertext sets n = blob.batch_count (separate outer field, bounded by MAX_BATCH_COUNT) -> ec_pve_batch_t pve_ct(n) (constructor resizes Q to n) -> coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) -> ec_pve_batch_t::convert: pre-check Q.size()==n passes (still n), then converter.convert(Q,...) clears and resizes Q to the attacker's inner length prefix m (m can be < n) -> get_Qs() curve loop iterates only the m present entries (no rejection) -> pve_ct.decrypt(..., skip_verify=true) -> for each kappa row, base_pke.decrypt(dk, inner_label, rows[i].c, x_buf): attacker can craft rows[i].c as a valid base-PKE ciphertext under the public ek so this returns SUCCESS for some row -> restore_from_decrypted(i, x_buf, curve, xs): builds x0,x1 of size n via bn_t::vector_from_bin, then loop `for (int i=0;i<n;i++){ x[i]=x0[i]+x1[i]; if (Q[i] != x[i]*G) ... }` reads Q[i] for i up to n-1 while Q.size()==m<n -> OOB std::vector<ecc_point_t> element whose embedded EC_POINT* pointer is uninitialized/out-of-bounds heap memory, then dereferenced inside ecc_point_t::operator!= (OpenSSL EC_POINT_cmp).
findings.impact: A malicious counterparty who supplies the serialized batch-PVE ciphertext can force the honest party's decrypt_batch (or get_public_keys_compressed_batch / decrypt_batch_*_hsm, which share the same construct+deserialize pattern) to read past the end of the Q vector and dereference a wild EC_POINT* in OpenSSL. At minimum this is a remotely triggerable crash (DoS) of the honest decryptor; because the wild pointer is consumed as an OpenSSL EC_POINT object, the concrete impact ranges from segfault to controlled OOB read depending on heap layout. No honest-party secret is leaked, but it is reachable purely through the public API boundary with valid base-PKE rows.
findings.poc_sketch: 1) Honest party generates (ek,dk) via generate_base_pke_*_keypair and publishes ek. 2) Attacker calls encrypt_batch(curve, ek, label, xs) with xs of size m (e.g. m=1) to obtain a well-formed pve_batch_ciphertext_blob_v1_t whose inner ct serializes a valid ec_pve_batch_t with Q.size()==1 and valid rows[i].c under ek. 3) Attacker edits only the OUTER blob field batch_count from 1 to a larger n (e.g. 50000), leaving the inner ct (Q vector length prefix = 1, valid c rows) unchanged; re-serialize. 4) Honest party calls decrypt_batch(curve, dk, ek, attacker_blob, label, out_xs). parse sets n=50000; constructor sizes Q to 50000; deserialization overwrites Q with the 1-element inner vector; skip_verify decrypt reaches restore_from_decrypted which loops i in [0,50000) reading Q[i] far past the 1 valid element -> OOB read / crash. Honest party runs unmodified code; attacker only crosses the public ciphertext boundary.
findings.attacker_role: Malicious party that produces the batch-PVE ciphertext blob consumed by an honest decryptor via the public decrypt_batch API.

result.attack_at_boundary: Malicious counterparty supplies the serialized batch-PVE ciphertext blob; honest party calls the public API coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch (include/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_single_recipient.h). The malicious party interacts only through the public API boundary, consistent with BUG_BOUNTY.md scope (include/cbmpc/api/).
result.reason: Verified against the real code. (1) Deserialization no-op pre-check confirmed: ec_pve_batch_t::convert (include-internal/.../pve_batch.h:48-61) checks `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` while Q is still constructor-sized n (passes), THEN calls converter.convert(Q,L,b). The std::vector overload in convert.h:114-131 does value.clear() then value.resize(count) where count is read from the wire (capped only by MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS=2^20, so attacker can pick m<n). So Q.size() becomes attacker-controlled m, decoupled from the n member. (2) Public-API reachability confirmed: decrypt_batch (src/.../pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:139) sets n=blob.batch_count, builds pve_ct(n), runs coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) which uses the single-arg convert (convert.h:276) that does NOT enforce no-trailing-bytes, then calls pve_ct.decrypt(..., skip_verify=true). The only loop over Q (lines 165-167) checks curve, not size. (3) verify() (pve_batch.cpp:74-78,86) DOES enforce size via `Q.size()!=n` and `Q!=this->Q`, but the decrypt path bypasses verify (skip_verify=true) and never re-checks Q.size(). restore_from_decrypted (pve_batch.cpp:144-184) validates row_index and buffer sizes but never Q.size(); its loop `for(i=0;i<n;i++){...; if(Q[i]!=x[i]*G)...}` indexes Q (size m) up to n-1 via unchecked operator[], an OOB std::vector<ecc_point_t> read. (4) Trigger feasibility confirmed: base PKE is standard KEM/DEM public-key crypto (pve_base.h:50-82); ek is public and supplied by the attacker, so the attacker encrypts chosen plaintexts that the honest dk decrypts back exactly, controlling x_buf size/content; inner_label=genPVELabelWithPoint(label,Q) (pve_base.h:254) is computable by the attacker. With m=0 (inner Q length prefix 0, n>=1) the OOB occurs on the very first iteration Q[0] as soon as any row decrypts to an n*curve_size buffer — no equality needs to be satisfied. ecc_point_t OOB element interprets adjacent heap as {ecurve_t curve; EC_POINT* ptr;} and operator!= -> operator== (base_ecc.cpp:909-917) derefs curve.ptr->equ_points when the bytes are non-null, i.e., wild EC_POINT* into OpenSSL. Impact: remotely-triggerable crash/DoS of the honest decryptor, possibly controlled OOB read depending on heap layout; no key-material disclosure. Same construct+deserialize-without-size-recheck pattern is shared by get_public_keys_compressed_batch and get_Label_batch (which iterate only the m present entries, so those are non-OOB but show the missing-validation pattern). Confidence is high but not maximal because a precise PoC must construct a fully self-consistent serialized blob (correct length prefixes for the 128 rows, a base-PKE ciphertext under the real RSA/ECIES KEM that decrypts to the right-sized buffer, and matching inner_label); these are all attacker-constructible but not yet demonstrated end-to-end here.

result.attack_at_boundary: Malicious counterparty supplies the serialized batch-PVE ciphertext blob consumed by the honest decryptor through the public API coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch(...) (and decrypt_batch_rsa_oaep_hsm / decrypt_batch_ecies_p256_hsm, which call into the same path). The honest party holds dk; the attacker only needs the public ek.
result.reason:
Verified against pristine upstream cb-mpc (commit e97eec7, clean working tree, origin coinbase/cb-mpc).

CORE CLAIM CONFIRMED. The inner Q vector length (m) is decoupled from the outer batch_count (n) and is never re-checked on the decrypt path:

1) Outer n is fixed by the constructor: decrypt_batch sets n = blob.batch_count (bounded 1..MAX_BATCH_COUNT=100000) and constructs ec_pve_batch_t pve_ct(n), whose ctor does Q.resize(n) and stores this->n = n. Deserialization never touches this->n.

2) The pre-check is a genuine no-op (pve_batch.h:48-52). On deserialization Q.size()==n at entry (ctor-sized), so `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` passes, THEN `converter.convert(Q, L, b)` runs. The generic std::vector<T> overload (convert.h:114-131) does value.clear(); reads attacker length-prefix count=m; only checks count > MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS (1<<20); then value.resize(m). So Q.size() becomes attacker-controlled m, with 0 <= m < n fully achievable. coinbase::convert(T&,mem_t) (convert.h:276-282) does NOT enforce trailing-byte/offset==size, so a short Q deserializes cleanly.

3) No re-validation on the decrypt path. The only Q.size()==n check in the codebase is verify() at pve_batch.cpp:78, and decrypt_batch calls decrypt(..., skip_verify=true) so verify is skipped. The post-deserialize loop `for (auto& q : pve_ct.get_Qs())` (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:165-167) iterates only the m present entries (curve check only) — no count check. restore_from_decrypted (pve_batch.cpp:144-187) validates only row_index and buffer sizes (against n*curve_size), never Q.size().

4) OOB read is reachable. restore_from_decrypted loops `for (int i=0;i<n;i++){ x[i]=x0[i]+x1[i]; if (Q[i] != x[i]*G) return E_CRYPTO; }` (pve_batch.cpp:181-184). x0 (DRBG, size n) and x1 (vector_from_bin requires x1_bin.size==n*curve_size -> size n; base_bn.cpp:536-549) are both size n. The attacker, as ciphertext producer, fully controls Q (m points), b (selects bi per row), row.r and row.c. Choosing bi=0 makes x1_bin the base-PKE plaintext of row.c — and since ek is public, the attacker encrypts a chosen n*curve_size plaintext under ek so base_pke.decrypt succeeds and yields that plaintext. They then set Q[i]=x[i]*G for i in [0,m), so the equality holds for the first m iterations and the loop advances to i=m, reading Q[m] past the end of a std::vector<ecc_point_t> of size m. The OOB-read element's embedded curve/EC_POINT* pointers are then consumed by ecc_point_t::operator!= -> operator== (base_ecc.cpp:909-917): `if(!ptr) return val.ptr==nullptr; if(!curve) return false; return curve.ptr->equ_points(...)` -> deref of out-of-bounds-read pointers (wild EC_POINT* / curve vtable).

5) Not intended design. The header doc disclaims only secret-leakage ("invalid ciphertexts ... designed to not leak secret information"), not memory safety; an OOB read/wild-pointer deref is outside that disclaimer.

Impact: remotely triggerable OOB read and wild-pointer dereference in the honest decryptor reachable through the public API with a valid base-PKE row; at minimum a DoS crash, with potential for controlled OOB depending on heap layout. No honest-party secret is disclosed. Medium is appropriate.

Minor inaccuracy in candidate (does not affect the core finding): the cited get_public_keys_compressed_batch path is NOT OOB — it iterates the m-sized Q vector directly (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:265) and does not loop to n. Only the decrypt path (and the HSM decrypt variants delegating to it) hits the n-bounded loop.

Verified against the real cb-mpc code; every load-bearing claim holds.

ROOT CAUSE (confirmed): converter_t's std::vector overload (include-internal/cbmpc/internal/core/convert.h:114-131) does value.clear() then value.resize(count) using the attacker's length prefix (bounded only by MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS=1<<20). In ec_pve_batch_t::convert (include-internal/cbmpc/internal/protocol/pve_batch.h:48-61) the guard `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` runs BEFORE converter.convert(Q,L,b) while Q is still constructor-sized n (pve_batch.h:13 Q.resize(n)). So the guard is a genuine no-op, and convert() then sets Q.size() = attacker count m. The outer loop bound n stays = blob.batch_count, a SEPARATE field (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:159,162), decoupled from Q.size().

BYPASS OF EXISTING CHECKS (confirmed): the only Q.size()==n enforcement is in verify() (pve_batch.cpp:78), but decrypt_batch calls decrypt with skip_verify=true (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:173-174 -> pve_batch.cpp:193 short-circuits verify). The post-deser curve loop (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:165-167) only iterates the m present get_Qs() entries, so it does not detect m<n. coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) (convert.h:276-282) does NOT enforce the strict trailing-byte check that deser() has, and the blob is fully attacker-constructible anyway (length-prefix m, m points, L, b, then kappa=128 fixed rows).

OOB + DEREF (confirmed): restore_from_decrypted (pve_batch.cpp:181-184) loops for(i=0;i<n;i++) and reads Q[i] while Q.size()==m<n. x0/x1 (bn_t::vector_from_bin resizes to n, base_bn.cpp:547) and xs (resize(n)) are all size n and safe; only Q is short, so Q[i] is an unchecked std::vector::operator[] OOB read of an ecc_point_t. ecc_point_t::operator!= -> operator== (base_ecc.cpp:909-917) reads this->ptr/this->curve from the OOB struct and calls curve.ptr->equ_points(...), dereferencing a wild EC_POINT*/curve pointer.

REACHABILITY (confirmed achievable): reaching restore_from_decrypted requires base_pke.decrypt to return SUCCESS for >=1 of the 128 rows (pve_batch.cpp:200). The base PKE is RSA-OAEP-2048 / ECIES-P256 (pve_base_pke.cpp); the malicious party holds the PUBLIC ek and controls both Q and label, so it can compute inner_label=genPVELabelWithPoint(label,Q) (sha256 of attacker's m-point Q) and forge a validly-decrypting row. No stdlib hardening turns operator[] into a checked access (compilation_flags.cmake: no _GLIBCXX_ASSERTIONS/libc++ hardening; _FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 does not cover vector::operator[]), and the c_api catch(...) (c_api/pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp) does not catch a SIGSEGV.

IMPACT: a remotely-triggerable OOB read + wild EC_POINT* dereference on the honest decryptor, reachable purely through the public decrypt_batch / decrypt_batch_rsa_oaep_hsm / decrypt_batch_ecies_p256_hsm API by a malicious counterparty supplying the ciphertext blob. Crash/DoS is certain; controlled OOB read is plausible depending on heap layout. No honest secret is leaked.

MINOR OVERSTATEMENT (does not refute): the candidate lists get_public_keys_compressed_batch / get_Label_batch as equally vulnerable. They are NOT — they only iterate the m-sized get_Qs() and never index to n, so they cannot trigger the OOB. Only the decrypt_batch family is exploitable. Core finding stands.
result.attack_at_boundary: Deserialization trust boundary: coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch (and decrypt_batch_rsa_oaep_hsm / decrypt_batch_ecies_p256_hsm) consuming the untrusted serialized pve_batch ciphertext blob supplied by a malicious counterparty. The malicious-party deviation: serialize ec_pve_batch_t with an inner Q length-prefix m strictly less than the outer batch_count n, with all 128 rows present and x_bin sized n*curve_size, and at least one row.c a valid base-PKE ciphertext under the public ek and inner_label=sha256(serialized m-point Q). The honest decryptor (skip_verify=true) deserializes Q to size m, decrypts that row, enters restore_from_decrypted, and reads Q[i] for i up to n-1 (OOB), dereferencing a wild EC_POINT* inside ecc_point_t::operator!=.

result.executive_summary:
One finding survived verification against pristine upstream coinbase/cb-mpc at commit e97eec7 (origin github.com/coinbase/cb-mpc, clean tree). It is REAL, REACHABLE through an in-scope public API, and NOT covered by the existing design disclaimer. It is a memory-safety bug (OOB std::vector read + wild EC_POINT* / curve-pointer dereference), not a key-compromise bug.

Core: in the batch-PVE decrypt path, the outer batch_count (n, the std::vector loop bound) is decoupled from the inner length-prefixed Q vector (size m) and is never re-validated on decrypt. A malicious counterparty supplies a serialized batch-PVE ciphertext blob whose outer batch_count is n (e.g. up to MAX_BATCH_COUNT=100000) but whose inner Q vector deserializes to m < n (e.g. m=0 or m=1). decrypt_batch runs with skip_verify=true, so the only Q.size()==n check (in verify(), pve_batch.cpp:78) is bypassed, and restore_from_decrypted (pve_batch.cpp:181-184) loops i in [0,n) reading Q[i] while Q.size()==m, an unchecked operator[] OOB read of a std::vector<ecc_point_t>. The OOB element's bytes are then consumed by ecc_point_t::operator!= -> operator== (base_ecc.cpp:909-914), which dereferences the out-of-bounds curve.ptr and EC_POINT*.

I verified every load-bearing link: the std::vector deserialization overload (convert.h:114-131) clear()+resize(m) with attacker-controlled m bounded only by MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS=2^20; the pre-check at pve_batch.h:48-52 is a genuine no-op (runs while Q is still ctor-sized n); coinbase::convert(T&,mem_t) (convert.h:276-282) does NOT enforce trailing-byte rejection unlike deser(); x0/x1/xs are all safely size n via vector_from_bin (base_bn.cpp:536-549) so only Q is short; base PKE is standard KEM/DEM public-key crypto (pve_base.h:50-82) so the attacker holding the public ek can forge a row whose base_pke.decrypt SUCCEEDS and reach restore_from_decrypted; and release builds carry only -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 (no _GLIBCXX_ASSERTIONS / libc++ hardening, cmake/compilation_flags.cmake:29) so operator[] is genuinely unchecked. The header doc (pve_batch_single_recipient.h:43-47) discloses only that invalid ciphertexts will not leak secrets; it does not disclaim memory safety, so this is outside intended behavior.

Tier assessment: this is reachable purely through the public API boundary by a malicious party who only crosses the serialized-ciphertext interface, with >=1 honest party (the decryptor) running unmodified code. Impact is a remotely-triggerable crash/DoS of the honest decryptor, with a plausible-but-unproven controlled-OOB-read upside depending on heap layout. No honest-party secret is disclosed and there is no demonstrated key compromise or RCE. I map it to Medium ($2k), conservatively, with a clear note that if an end-to-end PoC demonstrates only an unreliable/hard-to-control crash on a process that is not a persistent service, a triage team could argue Low; and that High would require demonstrating attacker-controlled OOB content flow (e.g. info-leak or control-flow influence), which is not established here.

The one candidate overstatement (that get_public_keys_compressed_batch / get_Label_batch are equally vulnerable) is refuted: those iterate the m-sized get_Qs() directly and never index to n, so they are not OOB. Only the decrypt_batch family (including the two HSM variants that delegate to it) is exploitable. This does not weaken the core finding.

ranked_findings.title: Batch-PVE decrypt: inner Q vector length not re-checked against batch_count on the skip_verify path -> OOB std::vector<ecc_point_t> read + wild EC_POINT*/curve-pointer dereference on the honest decryptor
ranked_findings.vuln_class: Out-of-bounds read on deserialized container (missing length re-validation) leading to wild-pointer dereference (CWE-125 / CWE-822)
ranked_findings.public_api_entry: coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch(curve_id, dk, ek, ciphertext, label, out_xs) in include/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_single_recipient.h (also reachable via decrypt_batch_rsa_oaep_hsm and decrypt_batch_ecies_p256_hsm, which delegate to the same function)
ranked_findings.why: Verified end-to-end against upstream cb-mpc @ e97eec7. (1) Decoupling: convert.h std::vector<T> overload (lines 114-131) on read does value.clear() then value.resize(count) where count is the attacker's wire length-prefix, bounded only by MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS=1<<20; so Q.size() becomes attacker-controlled m, independent of the member n. (2) Pre-check is a no-op: ec_pve_batch_t::convert (include-internal/.../pve_batch.h:48-52) tests `if(int(Q.size())!=n)` while Q is still constructor-sized n (pve_batch.h:13 Q.resize(n)), so it passes, THEN converter.convert(Q,L,b) shrinks Q to m. (3) Non-strict deser: decrypt_batch uses coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) (convert.h:276-282) which, unlike deser() (convert.h:254-266), does NOT reject trailing bytes, so a short Q deserializes cleanly. (4) verify() bypass: the only Q.size()==n enforcement is verify() (pve_batch.cpp:78), and decrypt_batch calls decrypt(...,skip_verify=true) (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:173-174), so verify is short-circuited (pve_batch.cpp:193). The post-deser curve loop (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:165-167) only iterates the m present get_Qs() entries, so it cannot detect m<n. restore_from_decrypted (pve_batch.cpp:144-187) validates only row_index and buffer sizes, never Q.size(). (5) OOB: restore_from_decrypted loops `for(int i=0;i<n;i++){ x[i]=x0[i]+x1[i]; if(Q[i]!=x[i]*G)...}` (181-184) reading Q[i] up to n-1 while Q.size()==m; x0/x1 are size n (vector_from_bin, base_bn.cpp:536-549, strictly checks n*size==mem.size and resizes to n), xs is resized to n in decrypt (pve_batch.cpp:192) -- so ONLY Q is short. Q[i] is std::vector::operator[], unchecked: cmake/compilation_flags.cmake carries only -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 (no _GLIBCXX_ASSERTIONS / libc++ hardening), so operator[] is not bounds-checked. (6) Deref: the OOB element interpreted as ecc_point_t{ecurve_t curve; EC_POINT* ptr} flows into operator!= -> operator== (base_ecc.cpp:909-914): if the OOB-read ptr and curve bytes are non-null it calls curve.ptr->equ_points(...), dereferencing a wild EC_POINT*/curve pointer. (7) Reachability: base PKE is a standard KEM/DEM public-key scheme (pve_base.h:50-82, RSA-OAEP-2048 / ECIES-P256); the attacker holds the PUBLIC ek and controls Q,label so computes inner_label=genPVELabelWithPoint(label,Q)=label+'-'+hex(sha256(Q)) (pve_base.h:253-256) and forges a row.c that base_pke.decrypt accepts -> restore_from_decrypted is entered. With m=0 the OOB hits on the very first iteration Q[0] regardless of equality. Scope: entry is coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch in include/cbmpc/api/ (in scope); demo-* and c_api/* not relied on as entry. Intended-behavior check: the header doc (pve_batch_single_recipient.h:43-47) disclaims only secret-leakage on invalid ciphertexts, NOT memory safety, so the OOB is out of intended scope. Confidence is high but not 1.0 because no end-to-end PoC blob has been built here: the attacker must assemble a fully self-consistent serialized ec_pve_batch_t (correct length prefix m for Q, valid L/b, and 128 row_t entries with at least one row.c a real RSA/ECIES KEM-DEM ciphertext under ek that decrypts to a buffer of the size restore_from_decrypted expects). All inputs are attacker-constructible, but the crash's reliability and any controlled-OOB upside depend on heap layout.
ranked_findings.suggested_next_step: Build the minimal independent-parties PoC (below) and run the honest decryptor under AddressSanitizer to capture the exact OOB read on Q and the subsequent wild deref in ecc_point_t::operator==. Then report to Coinbase with the ASan trace. Recommended fix to suggest: in ec_pve_batch_t::convert (post-deserialization) or at the top of decrypt()/restore_from_decrypted(), enforce `if (int(Q.size()) != n) return E_BADARG/set_error()` AFTER reading Q (the existing pre-read check is structurally a no-op); equivalently add a size re-check in decrypt_batch right after coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) before any use of get_Qs().

recommended_priority_order: Reproduce finding #1 end-to-end via include/cbmpc/api/ only: (1) honest party generates (ek,dk) and publishes ek; (2) attacker calls encrypt_batch(curve, ek, label, xs) with a single scalar (m=1) to obtain a well-formed pve_batch_ciphertext_blob_v1_t; (3) attacker re-serializes a tampered blob keeping the inner ct (Q length-prefix = 1 or 0, valid 128 rows) but setting the OUTER batch_count field to a large n (e.g. 50000); (4) honest party runs unmodified decrypt_batch(curve, dk, ek, tampered_blob, label, out_xs). Run the honest side under ASan to capture the Q[i] OOB read and the wild deref. Easiest crash variant: craft m=0 so Q[0] is OOB on the first loop iteration as soon as any row decrypts.

Confirm crash reliability and whether the OOB content is attacker-influenceable (heap-grooming) to decide between Medium and a possible High up-tier; if only an unreliable crash on a short-lived process is shown, pre-empt a Low down-tier argument with a service-context note.

Submit to Coinbase cb-mpc bounty with the ASan trace, the exact code citations, and the suggested one-line fix (re-validate Q.size()==n after deserialization / before the n-bounded loop). Explicitly scope OUT the get_public_keys_compressed_batch / get_Label_batch paths as non-vulnerable to avoid an over-claim that triage could use to discount the report.
result.coverage_and_caveats:
SCOPE OF THIS REVIEW: I verified the single supplied finding against the real source at C:\bugBounty\targets\cb-mpc on upstream coinbase/cb-mpc commit e97eec7 (git remote origin = github.com/coinbase/cb-mpc). I did NOT do an independent sweep for other bugs; this report covers only the provided candidate.

NOTE ON FILE/LINE: The finding header cites src/cbmpc/protocol/pve_batch.cpp:183, which is correct in this tree (the OOB read `if (Q[i] != x[i]*G)` is at pve_batch.cpp:183, inside restore_from_decrypted). The public-API plumbing lives in src/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp and the deserialization mechanics in include-internal/cbmpc/internal/core/convert.h and include-internal/cbmpc/internal/protocol/pve_batch.h. All four files were read in full for the load-bearing lines.

WHAT IS SOLID (directly confirmed in code): the n-vs-m decoupling; the no-op pre-check; the non-strict coinbase::convert vs strict deser distinction; the verify() bypass on skip_verify=true; x0/x1/xs all safely sized n so only Q is OOB; operator[] is unchecked under the project's actual compile flags (-D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 only); operator== dereferences the OOB element's curve/EC_POINT* pointers; base PKE is a standard public-key KEM/DEM so a malicious holder of the public ek can forge a decrypting row; MAX_CIPHERTEXT_BLOB_SIZE=64MiB (mem_util.h:17) is not an obstacle since a tampered blob with large outer batch_count but tiny inner Q is only a few KB; the public API entry is in include/cbmpc/api/ (in scope) and the honest party runs unmodified code with the attacker only crossing the serialized-ciphertext boundary.

DEEPEST UNCERTAINTY (why confidence is 0.88, not higher, and why poc_required=true for this Medium): (1) No end-to-end serialized blob has been constructed and executed here. The exact wire layout of ec_pve_batch_t::convert is: length-prefixed Q (m points) then L then b (buf128_t), then for each of kappa=128 rows the triple (x_bin, r, c). A working PoC must produce a self-consistent blob where at least one row.c is a genuine RSA-OAEP-2048 or ECIES-P256 KEM-DEM ciphertext under the published ek with inner_label=label+'-'+hex(sha256(serialized m-point Q)), and whose decrypted buffer satisfies restore_from_decrypted's size gate for that row's bi bit (either r01.size==16 for bi=1, or x1_bin.size==n*curve_size for bi=0). The m=0 / m=1 crash path is reachable, but I have not byte-assembled and run it, so the harness-level reproducibility is asserted from code reading, not demonstrated. (2) Severity sensitivity: the certain outcome is a crash/DoS of the honest decryptor; the 'controlled OOB read' upside is heap-layout dependent and UNPROVEN. If a PoC shows only a non-deterministic crash (e.g. the OOB ecc_point_t happens to read zeroed memory -> operator== returns early via `if(!ptr)` with no deref, yielding a clean E_CRYPTO rather than a fault on some heap states), a triage team could argue the bug is intermittently triggerable; that is why I held to Medium rather than High and flagged a possible Low down-tier risk. (3) Process-context dependence: bounty value of a DoS depends on whether the honest decryptor runs as a long-lived service vs a one-shot CLI; the repo alone does not settle that. (4) The candidate's claim that get_public_keys_compressed_batch / get_Label_batch are equally vulnerable is REFUTED here (they iterate the m-sized get_Qs() and never index to n); reporting them as vulnerable would be an over-claim and is excluded. NULL-RESULT NOTE: no key-compromise, key-recovery, RCE, or secret-leakage path was found in or around this finding; the impact ceiling on current evidence is memory-safety/DoS, hence the Medium ($2k) mapping rather than High/Critical.
